titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Dean and Fanny Wickes Papers
titlestmt.author: Martha Lund Smalley

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday September 2, 2020 at 22:18

did.unittitle: Dean and Fanny Wickes Papers
did.abstract: Dean and Fanny Wickes were missionaries serving in Shandong from 1912 to 1934 under the North China Mission of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions. Correspondence, writings, photographs, and collected material document the life and work of these missionaries who were engaged in educational and evangelistic work.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Dean and Fanny Wickes Papers (RG 257), Special Collections, Yale Divinity School Library.

chronitemset.event: Dean Rockwell Wickes born in Granville, Ohio
Graduated from the University of Chicago, having received a Senior College Scholarship for excellence in the Department of Geology, and Phi Beta Kappa (1904)
Received Master's degree from Yale University
Received B.D. from Yale Divinity School
Received Ph.D. from the University of Chicago, with focus on New Testament and Religious Education
Married Fanny Rollinson Sweeny, daughter of Mr. and Mrs. Louis LaBaume Sweeny

Dean and Fanny appointed missionaries of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM) and sailed to China, where they were stationed first in Beijing (Peking) for language study and then at the ABCFM station in T'ungchou/T'unghsien [Tongxian] as of September, 1913, where Dean taught at North China Union College. Summers were spent at the missionary retreat area in Beidaihe (Peitaiho).
Daughter Alice born
Family based in Beijing (Peking), Dean teaching at Theological College
Family on furlough in U.S., Dean studying at Union Theological Seminary, New York
Return to China; family based in Tongzhou Qu (T'unghsien)
Death of infant daughter Mary Frances
Birth of daughter Margaret
Family moved to Linqing (Lintsing)
Birth of daughter Eleanor
Furlough in U.S.; Dean studying at Union Theological Seminary, New York, received S.T.M. degree
Return to China; based in Linqing (Lintsing); Dean at Ellis Layman's Christian Training School
Family moves to Dezhou (Tehchow) as Linqing (Lintsing) station closes
Family informed that ABCFM can no longer support them in China; Dean returns to U.S. and finds job at Library of Congress
Fanny and Eleanor in in Los Angeles; move to Washington, D.C. in August; Dean is employed by Research Division of Soil Erosion, Department of Agriculture
Death of Fanny Wickes

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Dean and Fanny Wickes papers provide valuable documentation of their work as missionaries of the North China Mission of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions during the years 1912 to 1934. The Wickes were well-educated individuals dedicated to their work in China. Their letters home to their parents are detailed and substantial, with a focus on recounting their daily activities and surroundings in China.The Wickes papers complement other collections held at the Yale Divinity Library, including fellow North China Mission missionaries Earle and Thelma Ballou, Constance Buell, Emery Ellis, Lewis and Lois Gilbert, James and Maude Hunter, Helen Kintner, Harold Matthews, Alice Reed, Olga Robinson, and Ernest Shaw.
The Family Correspondence of Series I is primarily from the Wickes to their parents, a regular series of detailed letters. General Correspondence includes letters to and from colleagues in China.
Series II provides fairly sparse biographical documentation but does include family photograph albums and statements by the Wickes regarding their faith and work.

The Mission-related Collected Material of Series III provides insight into issues and concerns of the American Board mission during this era of political disruption in China and financial stress for missions.

Series IV contains a number of interesting reflections by both Dean and Fanny Wickes on their work and on events and customs in China. Some of these writings were published and others prepared to inform their supporters in the U.S.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Place names were modernized in the description, with the name originally used in the collection material or in an older version of the finding aid in parenthesis: e.g. "Beijing (Peking)" or "Benin (Dahomey)".

corpname.part: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions

did.unittitle: Correspondence
Family correspondence
Dean and Fanny Wickes to/from their parents

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters are primarily from Dean and Fanny to their parents. In later years there are some letters of their daughter Alice and from the parents.

did.unittitle: Dean (DW) attending Yale Divinity School
DW at YDS: campus life, assignments, sermon preparation
DW at YDS: attending China mission class, working on thesis
DW graduates from YDS; visits to extended family members before return to Chicago
Re. travels in U.S. and enroute to China, with stop in Honolulu
Continuation of trip to China with stop in Japan, arrival in Beijing (Peking); language study
Daily life in China; exploration of Beijing (Peking)
Life in Beijing (Peking); Empress Dowager's death and funeral
Life in Beijing (Peking); to summer resort in Beidaihe (Peitaiho)
Summer activities in Beidaihe (Peitaiho)
Move to T'ungchou [Tongxian]; DW teaching singing class; accounts of Chinese customs
In T'ungchou: missionaries' "Pastimes Club", government unrest, Christmas celebrations
In T'ungchou: study, teaching, travel to other mission stations
Travel to Linqing (Lintsing), teaching in Chinese; District meeting in Beijing (Peking), travel to Beidaihe (Peitaiho)
In Beidaihe (Peitaiho); purchase of house there
Return to T'ungchou and teaching; Dean Wickes chairman of Music Committee; flooding in area; Christmas trip to Beijing (Peking)
Re. school life and teaching; severe dust storms in area
To Beijing (Peking) for District Association meetings; visit to Great Wall and Ming Tombs; Dean Wickes appointed superintendent of boys' school
Summer activities in Beidaihe (Peitaiho); return to T'ungchou
DW submits resignation as superintendent of boys' school following incident of dismissal of students for demanding a change of Classics teacher; FW travels to country station
In T'ungchou; visit to Forbidden City; copy of mandate issued by Yuan Shih Kai
FW visits country fair for evangelism; school life
Death of Yuan Shih Kai; to Beidaihe (Peitaiho) for summer
Return to T'ungchou; FW's mother arrives for visit
School life; preparations for holidays
FW's mother returns to U.S.; some students leave school to work in France and Britain during war; DW chairs committee to celebrate 50 years of Christian work in T'ungchou
College at T'ungchou to be moved to Beijing (Peking) as part of federated university; travel to Beidaihe (Peitaiho) for summer
Birth of daughter Alice on August 7
Re. life in T'ungchou; Alice's growth
T'ungchou changed to T'unghsien; evangelism trips
Dean Wickes trip to Paoti to pay laborers and evangelize; planning joint missions at University of Peking; travel to Beidaihe (Peitaiho) for summer
Dean Wickes returns to T'unghsien for Teachers' Institute; family moves to Beijing (Peking); Dean Wickes teaching at Theological College
Celebrations for peace in China and end of European war; teaching activities; plans for furlough
Teaching in Beijing (Peking); Provincial Association meetings; furlough plans
Return to U.S. for furlough; DW studying at Union Theological Seminary
DW studying at Union Theological Seminary
Return to China; stationed in T'unghsien with trips to countryside, including Chang Hsin Tien
DW in Tai Shui and elsewhere doing relief work; extreme poverty in area; daughter Mary Frances born
Dean Wickes ill with measles; continues relief work; to Beidaihe (Peitaiho) for summer
Based in T'unghsien with evangelistic trips to out-stations; whooping cough at station
Daughter Mary Frances dies of cerebral meningitis; family to Beidaihe (Peitaiho) for summer; Dean Wickes makes country trips; political and military unrest in area
Dean Wickes on country preaching trip; 50th anniversary of Baoding (Paotingfu) station; teaching
Planning summer conference in WoFoSsu; to Beidaihe (Peitaiho) for summer
Return to T'unghsien; birth of daughter Margaret on October 20
Dean Wickes visiting villages and market towns; sings with Peking Choral Society
Preaching trips to countryside
Association meetings; village visits
Family at Beidaihe (Peitaiho); Dean Wickes teaching summer school in Beijing (Peking); flooding in area
Return to T'unghsien; DW doing relief work following floods; political unrest adds to difficulties; DW resumes country trips
T'unghsien city police and Governor of Metropolitan District start Christian meetings for staff; Association meeting includes discussion re: Wickes family moving to Linqing (Lintsing) station
Dean Wickes resumes country trips; to Beidaihe (Peitaiho) for summer
Wickes family moves to Linqing (Lintsing) in October; Dean Wickes takes country trip
Training School for Laymen established; DW teaching a three year course
Based in Linqing (Lintsing); Dean Wickes teaching at Bible school; military unrest in area
Opening of training school for practical service in the community; 40th anniversary of mission work in Linqing (Lintsing)
Council meetings in Tianjin (Tientsin); Dean Wickes's book "Flowers of Peitaiho" published; to Beidaihe (Peitaiho) for summer
DW to summer conference in Tehchow; to Ching Ping to organize a church; daughter Eleanor born October 13
Furlough plans; women and children ordered to leave Linqing (Lintsing) due to incident in Nanjing (Nanking); Fanny and girls go to Tianjin (Tientsin), then Korea
DW remains in China to finish up work; family sails to U.S. from Kobe, Japan in June; travel to NY, visiting FW's family
DW studying at Union Seminary; family in Poughkeepsie
DW graduates from Union Seminary; family travels to CA and leaves for China
Based in Linqing (Lintsing); school resumes; Dean visits village to examine candidates for church membership; electricity installed in home
Dean Wickes to Beijing (Peking) for meetings; family to Beidaihe (Peitaiho); new building for training school; trip to country station
Summer conference; problems with bandits; teaching, family life
Dean at conference in Beijing (Peking); life in Linqing (Lintsing), includes letters from daughter Alice at North China American School to her grandparents
Dean Wickes to country for visit; Fanny and children to Beidaihe (Peitaiho) in May; new grain seeds bring better harvest
Dean Wickes returns to Linqing (Lintsing) for conference; illness prevents return to Beidaihe (Peitaiho); Fanny Wickes has operation in Beijing (Peking)
Military unrest in area; Fanny and girls return to Linqing (Lintsing); holiday celebrations
DW teaching; country trips; family life and illnesses
Dean Wickes teaching; letters from Alice; to Beidaihe (Peitaiho) for summer; mission-related meetings
Incl. letters regarding the Tehchow incident when Dr. Tucker killed a robber at mission hospital
Teaching and country trips; holiday celebrations
DW busy with committee meetings, travel
Planning for conference on 5 year movement emphasizing literacy and rural needs; DW supervising student trips to rural groups
Proposal to combine Linqing (Lintsing) and Tehchow stations; family to Beidaihe (Peitaiho), Dean Wickes in Linqing (Lintsing)
Dean Wickes teaches class at Cheeloo University; joins family in Beidaihe (Peitaiho); Fanny has surgery in Beijing (Peking) before return to Linqing (Lintsing)
DW establishes village school in Wang Chia Li Chuang; plan for health work in country center; holiday celebrations
Linqing (Lintsing) and Tehchow stations to be merged; Dean in Pi Hsueh Tien for extended session of classes
Approach of Japanese troops delays Council meeting; Alice's school closes early
Dean Wickes to Beijing (Peiping) for North China Council meeting; Linqing (Lintsing) personnel to move to Tehchow; family to Beidaihe (Peitaiho)
FW chairing Evangelistic Committee; family moves to Tehchow; DW makes country trip
Wickes informed that they are "withdrawn" from mission due to financial concerns
Planning for return to U.S.; DW sails first for job search; gets job at Library of Congress Division of Orientalia; FW and Eleanor sail to U.S.; Alice and Margaret remain in school in China
FW living in Los Angeles with DW's father; DW appointed to Research Division of Soil Erosion, Department of Agriculture
FW and girls move to Washington DC in August; FW dies in November
Re. DW and daughters in Washington; Alice attending Oberlin College; DW remarries
Dean and Fanny Wickes letters to each other
Early meetings; Fanny attending Vassar College
Married and living in China; travels, daily life while apart
Letters written from Linqing (Lintsing), T'ungchou, Beidaihe (Peitaiho)
Letters written from Linqing (Lintsing), Beidaihe (Peitaiho), T'ungchou
Letters written from Linqing (Lintsing), Beijing (Peiping)
DW in Chiang Chun Chai; FW shares news of their being dropped by mission; DW returns to U.S. to look for job; FW and Eleanor in California
DW appointed to Soil Erosion Service, Department of Agriculture; plans for FW and Eleanor to move to DC
Alice and Margaret arrive from China; DW finds house for family in DC; preparations for moving
Dean and Fanny Wickes to extended family
To/from FW's brother and sister
From uncles William Wickes and Tom Wickes
From aunts Alice, Belle, Emily, Fannie, Margaret, Mary, Lizzie and cousin Alice
General correspondence
Dean and Fanny Wickes to/from others
A-C: Arnold, Baldwin, Ballou, Chandler, Chang, Charlie, Cross
D-G: Dundes, Ewing, Fairfield, Fifield, Fleming, Grimes
H-Le: Hemingway, Hubbard, Hugh, Hummel, Jacoby, Latourette, Lewis
Li-M: Li, Lobenstine, Lois, Lyons, Martin, Murphy
N-St: Nelson, North, Palmer, Richards, Robinson, Stelle, Steward
Su-Y: Sun, Tsur, Tsen, Upton, Whitaker, While, William, Wilson, Winans, Yung
Letters of others
A-M: Alice, Ballou, Bergen, Cooke, Cross, Darrow, Fairfield, Graves, Grimes, Murphy
R-W: Robinson, Shaw, Stevens, Sweeney, William Wickes
Biographical Documentation and Memorabilia
Regarding marriage, education, Fanny's will, later employment
Travel to China, medical letters, personal and household accounts
Diary, lists of property left with missionaries, miscellaneous
Family photographs; Fanny, Dean, daughters, scenes - album and loose photos
Family photographs: Albums for Eleanor and Mary Frances
Chinese language materials
Newsclippings
Programs for concerts, etc.
Invitations; re. Vassar College
Memorabilia
American College Club Peking Songbook
Mission-related Collected Material
Reports of Lintsing Memorial Hospital (1928, 1929), Ellis Christian Training School; American Board statements and reports
Regarding evacuation of missionaries in 1927; ordination of Pastor Chi Kua Tung; record book of various schools and people
Writings and Notes
Notebooks
Impressions of early days in China, studying Chinese
Notes about conference speakers; ideas for work and policy
Notes about Chinese men; daily notes regarding work, village work in Pi Hsueh Tien
Essays/notes

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These are primarily typescript and handwritten notes and essays by Dean and Fanny Wickes, in some cases reports to their supporters. Authorship of some writings is unclear.

did.unittitle: Including "The Winter Palace of Peking", "In Peking", "Getting Started in the Work" by FW
Including "The Cult of Blood" (published in Peking Gazette) by Fanny Wickes, "The College and the Academy" (re: North China Union College), "The Work of the TungChou Station"
Including ""The Touch of the West", Report of Fanny Wickes's visit to Third Moon Fair; "Report on Educational Work in Tungchou", "Language and its Study", "Notes on General Conditions"

Including "Our Colleagues", "Soldiers and Christian Soldiers in China", "A Festive Evening in Tunghsien", "A Jingle of Chinese History", "Notes on Tungchou Temples", "The Everyday Life of a Common Chinese Woman"
Including newsletter from Goodrich Girls' School, Tongzhou Qu (Tunghsien), "Bound Feet and Free", "Peking Industries", sermons
Including "They Are Hungry for the Gospel of Jesus Christ", "Our Answer" (poem re. their withdrawal from mission), excerpts from letter to North China Council
